text: Hi, welcome to our booth. We are Group 106, and our sponsor is Murphy Oil, a U.S.-based upstream oil and gas company.
Our capstone focuses on analyzing their inspection data to understand what is really driving corrosion in their equipment.
More specifically, we looked at their horizontal separators and the piping circuits connected to them.
A horizontal separator is a large vessel that splits the production stream into oil, gas, and water,
so each stream can move on for further refining and distribution.
To identify those corrosion drivers, we started with manual analysis, using the literature to map the corrosion mechanisms we would expect to see.
Then we trained machine learning models on the same inspection data.
We gave the models hundreds of variables and asked them to predict percent wall loss in the piping.
Across the models, we reached about 86% accuracy, with an average error of 2.6%.
So in practice, the predicted wall-loss percentage is off by about 3% on average.
That might sound high, but for a problem this complex, those are actually strong results.
Machine learning is especially useful here because we can feed in the full data set instead of hand-testing every factor.
The models then learn the patterns hidden in that data.
After that, we open up the models and inspect which relationships matter most,
instead of manually sorting through thousands of possible parameter combinations.
Once we understood those correlations, the next step was to compare them with historical failure events.
At a high level, those failure records supported our main trends, including the regions and circuits most susceptible to corrosion.
But some of the more specific insights could not be confirmed, because the failure records did not include enough detail.
Finally, we brought all of those insights into a Power BI dashboard that lets Murphy Oil engineers explore the patterns and correlations.
It updates automatically whenever new inspection data comes in.
It also risk-ranks the equipment locations that may need more frequent inspection or closer attention,
based on a formula we created using the validated parameters we identified.
